name: Yank Lawson
abstract: John Rhea "Yank" Lawson (May 3, 1911 – February 18, 1995) was an American jazz trumpeter known for Dixieland and swing music. Born John Lausen in 1911, from 1933 to 1935 he worked in Ben Pollack's orchestra and after that became a founding member of the Bob Crosby Orchestra.

about_quotes.speaker: John Budd
about_quotes.evidence: Then we became friends. He said “come in” and he sat with me through © Hamilton College Jazz Archive -11- the evening until it closes at 3 AM
about_quotes.doc_title: John Budd interviewed by Monk Rowe, Aspen, Colorado, October 11, 1997 [transcript only]

about_quotes.speaker: Ralph Sutton
about_quotes.evidence: I’ll never forget when I was with Bob Haggart and Yank Lawson, and it was with a band, it was called the World’s Greatest Jazz Band.
about_quotes.doc_title: Ralph Sutton interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, September 22, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Bob Haggart
about_quotes.evidence: And we had a radio broadcast coming up as I remember it was at 5:00. And we used to put on a show for the kids with the small band, come down off the stand and had trumpet, trombone, clarinet, tenor, it was Yank Lawson and then Eddie Miller and Matty Matlock and Smitty.
about_quotes.doc_title: Bob Haggart interviewed by Michael Woods, Clinton, New York, September 22, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Dave McKenna
about_quotes.evidence: in the days when I worked at Condon’s with Yank Lawson and Peanuts Hucko... we did an album with Clancy, we did a couple of albums. Clancy was the leader on one and Yank was the leader on the other.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dave McKenna interviewed by Monk Rowe, Chautauqua, New York, September 12, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Cliff Leeman
about_quotes.evidence: I made the Clambake Seven songs with Yank Lawson... That was the begining of a beautiful thing --' recording wise and friendship wise, and a series of albums, running between 20 and 30.

about_quotes.speaker: Tim Kelly
about_quotes.evidence: But, it's a Dick Richardson band, really. But, Dean Kincaid was the arranger. Yank Lawson was one of the trumpeters. Wenge was one of the trumpeters.
